Title: Production of the antimalarial drug precursor artemisinic acid in engineered yeast

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: GAL1 promoter (inducible, for plasmid-borne ADS expression in yeast); chromosomal integration context for host-pathway engineering not always promoter-specified
Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: EPY201/208/210/213/219/224 engineering strains; YPH499 for microsome assays
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Use as a heterologous sesquiterpene cyclase to channel elevated FPP into amorpha-4,11-diene, the committed scaffold for artemisinic acid. Best deployed together with strong mevalonate-pathway upregulation and reduced sterol drain, then paired with CYP71AV1+CPR. Gene stacking with tHMGR, ERG9 repression, and optionally upc2-1 is recommended.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Introduces the first committed artemisinin-pathway step in yeast and enables production of amorphadiene from FPP; by itself yields low amorphadiene, but in an optimized FPP background supports high precursor accumulation.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in engineered yeast; GC-MS quantification of amorphadiene in shake-flask cultures across strain backgrounds.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Galactose induction; synthetic defined medium; methionine supplementation relevant to PMET3 repression in engineered backgrounds; culture at 30°C.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologously induced in yeast under GAL1/galactose conditions.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Not reported in detail here; ADS is described as a characterized artemisinin-biosynthetic enzyme from Artemisia annua.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Comparative genomic/EST analysis used for P450 discovery, but no accession given for ADS-specific omics.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ADS is the first committed enzyme of artemisinin biosynthesis and was used as a functional reporter for improved FPP supply during host-pathway engineering.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Without precursor-pathway optimization, product titre is low; strong flux draw from FPP may compete with native sterol metabolism and growth when combined with sterol-pathway repression.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ADS catalyses the first committed step from FPP to amorphadiene in artemisinin biosynthesis. 2. Expression of ADS in yeast enabled amorphadiene detection and served as a readout for mevalonate/FPP-pathway engineering. 3. Coupling ADS with host-pathway optimization increased amorphadiene to 153 mg l^-1, enabling downstream artemisinic acid synthesis.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Associated mainly with engineered yeast cell pellet/cell surface; removable by alkaline wash
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Immediate precursor for semisynthesis of artemisinin, an antimalarial drug effective against multidrug-resistant Plasmodium spp.

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native expression not engineered directly; host background carries GAL1-inducible ADS and PMET3-repressed ERG9 in some strains
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Key flux-amplification target for sesquiterpene engineering. Overexpress a truncated soluble HMGR copy, and consider multiple chromosomal integrations for stable high mevalonate flux. Combine with product-pathway genes and with ERG9 repression to maximize precursor availability.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Strongly increases mevalonate-pathway flux and boosts amorphadiene production; additional copy further increases titre.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Chromosomal overexpression of truncated HMGR in yeast backgrounds followed by GC-MS quantification of amorphadiene.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Galactose induction of downstream ADS; cultivation in yeast synthetic medium at 30°C.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A truncated soluble form of 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-CoA reductase was used; this bypasses native membrane-associated constraints and is a classical mevalonate-pathway engineering strategy.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overdriving mevalonate flux may impose metabolic burden and imbalance isoprenoid homeostasis, though specific penalties were not quantified here.
Common_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: Host endogenous gene, truncated engineered form
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Overexpression of truncated HMGR improved amorphadiene production about fivefold. 2. A second integrated copy further increased titre by ~50%. 3. tHMGR is a major precursor-supply lever for artemisinic acid pathway engineering in yeast.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Associated mainly with engineered yeast cell pellet/cell surface after downstream oxidation
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Immediate precursor for semisynthesis of artemisinin antimalarial drugs

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: PMET3 methionine-repressible promoter (inducible/repressible control used for downregulation)
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A prime competing-pathway target. Partial repression of ERG9 can redirect FPP away from sterol synthesis toward sesquiterpene products. Best used with precursor-pathway enhancement and careful tuning to avoid excessive sterol deficiency.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Downregulation increases amorphadiene production by reducing conversion of FPP into sterols.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Promoter replacement with PMET3 followed by methionine-mediated repression and GC-MS measurement of amorphadiene.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Methionine supplementation (1 mM) represses PMET3-driven ERG9 expression; culture at 30°C.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Engineered downregulation in yeast under methionine-repressible conditions.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ERG9 encodes squalene synthase, the first committed step after FPP in native sterol biosynthesis, making it a major sink competing with heterologous sesquiterpene production.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Because ERG9 is essential for sterol biosynthesis, excessive repression may impair growth or membrane function; the study used tunable repression rather than deletion.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ERG9 consumes FPP for sterol synthesis and competes directly with amorphadiene production. 2. PMET3-mediated downregulation roughly doubled amorphadiene titre. 3. Combining ERG9 repression with other pathway modifications was important for high-level precursor accumulation.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Associated mainly with engineered yeast cell pellet/cell surface after downstream oxidation
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Immediate precursor for semisynthesis of artemisinin antimalarial drugs

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native chromosomal overexpression context not promoter-specified
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can be included as a supporting precursor-supply gene when optimizing FPP flux, although effect here was modest relative to tHMGR and ERG9. Useful in balanced pathway stacks rather than as a sole intervention.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression had little effect on total amorphadiene titre, though specific production increased slightly due to reduced cell density.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Chromosomal overexpression followed by GC-MS quantification of amorphadiene and comparison of specific production.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Standard yeast culture and galactose induction of ADS.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ERG20 encodes FPP synthase; despite an intuitive role, it was not the principal bottleneck under these conditions.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Possible growth reduction/cell-density decrease when overexpressed; total titre gain was limited.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ERG20 overexpression alone was not a major determinant of total amorphadiene titre in the optimized background. 2. Specific productivity rose modestly. 3. ERG20 is supportive but not the dominant engineering leverage point in this study.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Associated mainly with engineered yeast cell pellet/cell surface after downstream oxidation
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Immediate precursor for semisynthesis of artemisinin antimalarial drugs

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: GAL1 promoter (inducible) on pRS425 for ADS expression in yeast
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Essential entry-point enzyme for reconstructing the artemisinin precursor pathway in microbes. Combine with FPP-pathway enhancement and downstream CYP71AV1+CPR oxidation module. Suitable for modular pathway stacking in industrial terpene-production chassis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables de novo amorphadiene formation from host FPP and thereby initiates heterologous artemisinic acid biosynthesis in yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in yeast; GC-MS detection and quantification of amorphadiene.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms the direct hydrocarbon precursor of the target product.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Not detailed here; enzyme isolated from Artemisia annua.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used both as a biosynthetic enzyme and as a functional reporter of FPP flux improvements in engineered yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Precursor drain from sterol metabolism limits output unless host flux is rebalanced.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ADS converts FPP to amorphadiene, the first committed intermediate toward artemisinic acid. 2. In an optimized yeast host, ADS supported up to 153 mg l^-1 amorphadiene. 3. ADS is indispensable for coupling engineered mevalonate flux to artemisinic acid production.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Engineered yeast cell pellet/cell surface; removable by alkaline wash
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Immediate precursor of artemisinin, a potent antimalarial drug

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Galactose-inducible promoters on pESC-URA for co-expression with CPR
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: EPY224 for in vivo production; YPH499 for microsome assays
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Artemisinin / artemisinic acid sesquiterpene oxidation pathway
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A central oxidation-module enzyme for microbial artemisinic acid production. Must be co-expressed with a compatible CPR. Strong candidate for pathway optimization by expression balancing, redox engineering, and membrane-environment tuning.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Performs three sequential oxidations at the C12 position of amorpha-4,11-diene: amorphadiene to artemisinic alcohol, artemisinic alcohol to artemisinic aldehyde, and artemisinic aldehyde to artemisinic acid.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Converts amorphadiene efficiently to artemisinic acid in vivo and in vitro, with negligible accumulation of intermediates, making yeast produce the immediate artemisinin precursor.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous co-expression with CPR in yeast; GC-MS identification of artemisinic acid matching plant standard; in vitro microsome assays using amorphadiene, artemisinic alcohol, and artemisinic aldehyde as substrates.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms the target product through terminal multi-step oxidation.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Galactose induction; microsomal assays with NADPH regeneration; culture at 30°C; alkaline wash revealed exported product association with cell surface.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologously induced in yeast under galactose-inducible promoters.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Recovered from a trichome-enriched cDNA pool of Artemisia annua.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Comparative EST/genomic analysis in Asteraceae used for discovery; GenBank accession DQ268763.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper argues CYP71AV1 is a key contributor to artemisinin biosynthesis in A. annua. Although additional dehydrogenases in plants cannot be excluded, recombinant CYP71AV1 alone catalysed all three oxidations efficiently in yeast microsomes.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Late oxidation module from sesquiterpene hydrocarbon to acid
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: P450 expression can impose redox and membrane burden; requires CPR co-expression. Specific growth penalties were not quantified.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Artemisinic acid (via artemisinic alcohol and artemisinic aldehyde intermediates)
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Amorpha-4,11-diene; also artemisinic alcohol and artemisinic aldehyde in stepwise assays
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP71AV1 was identified from Artemisia annua as a conserved Asteraceae P450 candidate linked to sesquiterpene-lactone metabolism. 2. Co-expression with CPR in yeast led to production of authentic artemisinic acid. 3. Microsome assays proved CYP71AV1 catalyses all three oxidation steps from amorphadiene to artemisinic acid.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Engineered yeast cell pellet/cell surface; largely removable by alkaline wash
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Immediate precursor for semisynthesis of artemisinin antimalarial drugs

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Galactose-inducible promoters on pESC-URA for co-expression with CYP71AV1
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Cytochrome P450 redox support within artemisinic acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Required redox partner for functional deployment of CYP71AV1 in yeast. Should be co-optimized with P450 expression for electron transfer efficiency; choice of cognate CPR can strongly affect titres.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers electrons from NADPH to cytochrome P450 enzymes, enabling CYP71AV1-catalysed oxidation of amorphadiene intermediates.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports CYP71AV1 activity and is necessary for artemisinic acid formation in the engineered oxidation module.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene isolation from A. annua; in vitro biochemical confirmation of oxidoreductase function (Km for cytochrome c and NADPH); co-expression with CYP71AV1 in yeast enabling artemisinic acid production.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required cofactor-supplying/redox partner for target-product formation.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologously induced in yeast under galactose-inducible promoters.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Isolated from Artemisia annua; detailed native pattern not reported.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although not itself forming artemisinic acid, CPR is indispensable for CYP71AV1 function in this heterologous system.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Adds redox demand on NADPH metabolism; may contribute to membrane-expression burden.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Reduced cytochrome P450 catalytic cycle support (no standalone pathway metabolite)
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A. annua CPR was isolated as the native redox partner for CYP71AV1. 2. Its oxidoreductase activity was confirmed biochemically in vitro. 3. Co-expression with CYP71AV1 enabled artemisinic acid production in yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Immediate precursor for semisynthesis of artemisinin antimalarial drugs

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Overexpression context not promoter-specified for mutant allele; ERG9 simultaneously under PMET3 in combined strains
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful as a host-level regulatory lever to broadly upregulate sterol/isoprenoid genes and support high FPP flux, especially in combination with partial ERG9 repression. Appropriate for chassis optimization rather than pathway specificity.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: The semi-dominant upc2-1 allele modestly increased amorphadiene alone, but strongly enhanced production when combined with ERG9 repression, contributing to 105 mg l^-1 amorphadiene.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression of mutant allele in engineered yeast backgrounds followed by GC-MS measurement of amorphadiene.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller that indirectly increases artemisinic acid precursor supply by transcriptionally activating sterol/isoprenoid biosynthetic capacity.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Methionine-repressible ERG9 context affects its apparent benefit; standard yeast culture with galactose-induced ADS.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Engineered overexpression of semi-dominant mutant allele in yeast.
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: ERG9 and multiple sterol biosynthesis genes are indirectly affected
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Precursor supply for sesquiterpene biosynthesis via global regulation of sterol/isoprenoid pathway genes
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: upc2-1 is a mutant allele of the global sterol-biosynthesis transcription factor UPC2; its value here is indirect pathway support rather than direct control of the heterologous artemisinin genes.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Global sterol-pathway rewiring may alter membrane composition or growth, though this was not quantified in detail.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Sterol biosynthesis genes (specific direct targets not enumerated in this paper)
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation via semi-dominant gain-of-function allele
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Enhances expression of sterol/isoprenoid biosynthetic genes, indirectly supporting FPP production
Regulation_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Nucleus (as transcription factor; not directly shown here)
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. upc2-1 alone had only a modest effect on amorphadiene production. 2. In combination with ERG9 repression, it substantially increased amorphadiene titre. 3. upc2-1 acts as a global regulatory enhancer of host isoprenoid precursor metabolism in this engineering strategy.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Engineered yeast cell pellet/cell surface after downstream oxidation
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Immediate precursor of artemisinin antimalarial drugs
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Mutant gain-of-function allele; native sterol-status signals not examined here
